one_line_takeaway: A pragmatic experience report showing that thin MCP adapters over mature Globus/HPC services let a general LLM agent orchestrate real multi-site scientific workflows—strong on engineering lessons and one retrieval benchmark, but with only qualitative reliability evidence and no security threat model despite its cluster label.
key_finding: Thin MCP adapters over existing research services let a general agent (Claude Desktop + Sonnet 4) plan and execute complex, multi-site scientific workflows by dynamically generating Python glue code, removing the need for hand-written integration. Concretely: agents recovered from some failures autonomously (reprocessing a Globus Compute Python error to fix a malformed invocation; resubmitting a Globus Transfer with a corrected path). For Rhea, incorporating richer textual context monotonically improves retrieval—embedding tool names alone performs worst, while adding descriptions, then extended documentation, then repository README files progressively raises Recall@k (best strategy 'Plus README' reaches roughly 0.8 Recall@10 in Figure 2). The quantum-chemistry case produced HOMO-LUMO gaps consistent with published literature. Limitation surfaced by the authors' own evaluation: agents made repetitive mistakes (limited in-session learning), sometimes failed to complete specified tasks, and produced inconsistent outputs (e.g., different visualizations for similar requests).
core_contribution: An experience/systems report on using the Model Context Protocol (MCP) as a unifying interface to expose heterogeneous scientific cyberinfrastructure (CI) to LLM agents. The authors' own contributions are: (1) reference implementations of thin MCP servers wrapping mature research services—Globus Transfer, Globus Compute, and Globus Search; a Computing Facility status server for ALCF and NERSC; the Octopus event fabric; the Garden scientific-ML model platform; and Rhea (a RAG-based server over the Galaxy Toolshed); (2) a formal problem definition modeling an agentic application as a three-stage Plan → Resolve → Execute workflow W(p)=Execute(Resolve(Plan(p,L),M,S),Φ_user), with servers defined as M_k=⟨C_k,Φ_k⟩ and capabilities as C_j=⟨I_j,E_j,D_j,R_j⟩; (3) four application case studies demonstrating the architecture; and (4) distilled lessons (favor thin adapters over new services; separate discovery from invocation for large tool ecosystems; let agents generate glue code on demand). Rhea's specific design—exposing a single find_tools capability that runs RAG over tool documentation and then dynamically materializes matching MCP tools via the protocol's notification channel—is a notable engineering contribution. NOTE: PaperQA2, Biomni (150 tools/59 databases/105 packages via LangChain), TaskMatrix.AI, ToolACE, and the 8000+ servers in the Glama directory are cited related work, NOT this paper's contributions.
threat_model: None. The paper does not define an adversary, attack surface, or security evaluation, despite the knowledge-graph cluster label 'MCP Agent Security.' Security enters only as an operational/engineering concern: the authors discuss OAuth 2.0 token management, refresh cycles, and scope management with Globus Auth, and choose to run MCP servers locally inside the user's trusted environment (wrapping Globus calls in an auth handler) to avoid the complexity of secure token passing in hosted deployments. They cite—but do not conduct—MCP security/privilege studies (refs [24] Hasan et al. on MCP server security/maintainability, [34] Li et al. on privilege management/API usage). There is no threat model to summarize; imposing one would misrepresent the paper.
limitations: (1) Evaluation is almost entirely qualitative and single-configuration—only Claude Desktop + Claude Sonnet 4, with no systematic comparison across agents or LLMs. (2) Only Rhea has a quantitative benchmark, and its 380 queries are LLM-generated (Llama-3.3-70B) with a single ground-truth tool per query, so it measures single-tool retrieval, not full workflow correctness; Recall values are read from a figure, not tabulated. (3) Reported agent resilience is anecdotal; the authors note agents repeated mistakes and gave inconsistent outputs, but no failure-rate or reliability metrics are provided. (4) Open, unsolved challenges the authors flag: hosting cross-administrative-domain MCP services under current authentication models, evaluating agent-workflow reliability, and strengthening resilience/recovery for long-running tasks. (5) A minor internal inconsistency: Prompt B.1 lists 10 bacterial species while the described output summary references 'six' analyzed species. (6) The MCP server code repository is stated as 'available soon,' limiting reproducibility at publication.

datasets_used: 380 natural-language queries generated by Llama-3.3-70B from Galaxy training tutorials (Rhea retrieval benchmark; ground-truth tool mappings derived from tutorial workflows, e.g., 'proteogenomics-1-database-creation' → gffcompare)
Galaxy Toolshed tool documentation (names, descriptions, help strings, parameter schemas, README files) as the RAG corpus
Copper crystal structures: one 32-atom structure (single relaxation) and a batch of 49 structures (Garden/MACE case study)
Six carbonate battery-electrolyte solvents — linear DMC, EMC, DEC and cyclic EC, PC, VC (quantum-chemistry case study)

Motor-protein sequences for 10 bacterial species (fliC/flaB/flaA/fliM genes across E. coli, P. aeruginosa, B. burgdorferi, S. marcescens, S. oneidensis, V. cholerae, L. monocytogenes, S. enterica, A. tumefaciens, C. difficile) retrieved via NCBI ESearch (phylogenetics case study)
Lustre filesystem change events streamed via Octopus and indexed in Globus Search (Icicle case study; 87 events sampled by the agent for the summary)
quantitative_results: Rhea retrieval: best embedding strategy ('Tool name + description + documentation + README') reaches ~0.8 Recall@10 over 380 queries; performance increases monotonically as name-only < +description < +documentation < +README (Figure 2)
Computed HOMO-LUMO gaps at M06-2X/6-311++G(d,p) via GPU4PySCF on ALCF Polaris (eV): DMC 10.26, EMC 10.16, DEC 10.07, EC 10.07, PC 9.97, VC 8.58
Garden/MACE: single 32-atom copper structure relaxed to final energy -130.71 eV with typical FCC lattice parameters; batch of 49 copper structures processed on ALCF Edith cluster
Phylogenetics: three reconstruction tools run (FastTree with JTT+CAT, IQ-TREE 2 with 1000 ultrafast bootstraps, RAxML PROTGAMMAJTT with 100 rapid bootstraps) across ALCF Polaris and NERSC Perlmutter
Icicle: agent sampled 87 Lustre events; retrieval requested up to 10,000 events within a 10-second timeout
baselines_compared: Internal ablation over four Rhea documentation-embedding strategies: tool name only vs. +description vs. +documentation vs. +README (no external retrieval-system baseline)
Computed HOMO-LUMO gaps compared qualitatively against published literature (Shakourian-Fard et al. [49]) and found consistent
No external agent/tool-serving systems (e.g., Biomni, PaperQA2, LangChain tool-calling) are empirically benchmarked against; they appear only as related-work discussion
